answer:
In the Smṛti corpus, *tīrtha* (तीर्थ; often Anglicized as *tirtha*) primarily denotes a sacred "ford," "crossing-place," or pilgrimage site—typically associated with rivers, confluences, lakes, mountains, or other natural loci—where ritual bathing (*snāna*), ancestral offerings (*śrāddha*), libations (*tarpaṇa*), or other rites confer purification from sins (*pāpa*), especially for ancestors (*pitṛs*).[^1] [^2] It embodies a liminal space for spiritual transition, metaphorically extending to "moving tīrthas" like righteous Brāhmaṇas.[^3] A secondary, ritual usage refers to the four "tīrthas" (sacred parts) of the hand employed in sipping water (*ācamana*) for purification: *Brahma-tīrtha* (base of thumb), *deva-tīrtha* (fingertips), *pitṛ-tīrtha* (between thumb and forefinger), and *prājāpatya-tīrtha* (palm base).[^4] [^5] Brāhmaṇas must sip using *Brahma*, *deva*, or *kaya-tīrtha*, avoiding *pitṛ-tīrtha*.[^6]

### Key Roles and Merits
- **Purification and Expiation**: Bathing at a *tīrtha* equals or exceeds penances (*prāyaścitta*, *kṛcchra*). E.g., 100 baths in a great river expiates abandoning ancestral rites; no expiation rivals bathing, especially at sacred fords.[^7] A *tīrtha*-bath equals *Vyāhṛtis* recitation or Vedic rites.[^8]
- **Ancestral Satisfaction (*Pitṛ-kṛpa*)**: *Śrāddhas* at *tīrthas* are "imperishable" (*akṣaya*), granting eternal bliss. E.g., Puṣkara *śrāddha* is *akṣaya*.[^9]
- **Hierarchy**: Gaṅgā > other rivers > tīrthas; Brāhmaṇas embody all tīrthas/rivers.[^10]

### Prominent Tīrthas Listed
Viṣṇu Smṛti 85 enumerates ~60+ *tīrthas* (many rivers/banks/confluences) where bathing/rites purify "from all sins," esp. for *śrāddha*:[^11]
- Puṣkara, Gayāśīrṣa (Vaṭa), Amarakaṇṭaka, Varāha mountain, Narmadā/Yamunā/Gaṅgā banks.
- Kuśāvarta, Binduka, Nīla mountain, Kanakhala, Kubjāmra, Bhṛgutuṅga, Kedāra.
- Prabhāsa, Sarasvatī, Gaṅgādvāra/Prayāga, Gaṅgā-ocean confluence.
- Naimiṣa forest, Vārāṇasī, Agastya/Kaṇva hermitages, Kauśikī/Sarayū, Śoṇa-Jyotiṣā.
- Godāvarī, Gomatī, Vetravatī, Vipāśā, Vitastā, Śatadrū, Candrabhāgā, Irāvatī, Sindhu, Pañcanada, etc.
Paraśara and Aṅgirasa echo river-baths (*snāna*) for expiation; pilgrimage (*tīrtha-yātrā*) equals penances.[^12] [^13]

The corpus (37 works; Smṛtis like Manu, Viṣṇu, Yājñavalkya, Paraśara, Aṅgirasa, etc.) emphasizes *tīrthas* for Kali Yuga efficacy, but not every text lists them exhaustively—focus is ritual/ethical utility over geography.

[^1]: Paraśara Smṛti, ch.12, [para.](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/12/translation/#p-756f13).
[^2]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti, ch.1, [para.](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-e61dcc).
[^3]: Paraśara Smṛti, ch.6, [para.](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/06/translation/#p-3f3769).
[^4]: Manu Smṛti, ch.2, [para.](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-b0b95e).
[^5]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, ch.1, [para.](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-d918cb).
[^6]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch.62, [para.](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/62/translation/#p-81316f).
[^7]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti, ch.1, [para.](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-a96e03).
[^8]: Ibid., [para.](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-e61dcc).
[^9]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch.85, [para.](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/85/translation/#p-a49b09).
[^10]: Paraśara Smṛti, ch.12, [para.](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/12/translation/#p-0e2f81); Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch.85, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/85/translation/#p-cf3e32).
[^11]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch.85, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/85/translation/#p-cf3e32).
[^12]: Paraśara Smṛti, ch.12, [passage](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/12/translation/#p-ce3b1c).
[^13]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti, ch.1, [para.](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-a96e03).
